metadata.split_role: Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
metadata.notes: Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. v4.2.81 added species-specific bolete notes/source links and simplified review reasons for selected records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

records.culinary_uses: A porcini-type edible when confidently identified and in good condition.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Almost Bluing King Bolete. Added in the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Weak or limited blue bruising possible
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_detail: Confirm pale pores, porcini-like build, mild taste, and limited/weak blue staining behavior; avoid any red-pored or bitter look-alikes.
records.field_identification: Porcini-like bolete with whitish to yellow pores, firm white flesh, brown cap, and possible faint/limited blue staining. Compare against bitter Tylopilus and red/yellow boletes.
records.edibility_notes: A porcini-type edible when confidently identified and in good condition.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added during the bolete expansion pass for the clean app; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

Future research: verify whether Almost Bluing King Bolete / Boletus subcaerulescens has any credible beverage/tea use; if so, add specific details and do not classify medicinal-only beverage use as ordinary edible food.

edible_use.notes: A porcini-type edible when confidently identified and in good condition.

records.culinary_uses: Bitter; not a food target. One bitter bolete can wreck a meal.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bitter Tylopilus with pale cap skin that can peel or fragment; useful to recognize so it does not ruin a basket of boletes.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; northeastern/Midwestern distribution noted in keys.
research_notes: White to pale cap, brown-staining stem, bitter taste, and a thin papery cap surface that may peel or fragment with age. In the Tylopilus group, taste is a spit-out clue only.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food because of bitterness and Tylopilus complexity.

records.review_notes: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage.
records.mushroom_card_note: Bitter Tylopilus with pale cap skin that can peel or fragment; useful to recognize so it does not ruin a basket of boletes.
records.field_identification: White to pale cap, brown-staining stem, bitter taste, and a thin papery cap surface that may peel or fragment with age. In the Tylopilus group, taste is a spit-out clue only.

records.culinary_uses: Often considered edible and one of the better Tylopilus-type boletes, but use only with confident ID.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Black velvet bolete: dark velvety cap and stem, white pores becoming pinkish, and flesh/pore bruising that turns red then black.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, east of the Rockies.
research_notes: Dark blackish velvety cap, dark stem, white pore surface becoming pinkish, and bruising that turns red then brown/black are the key field marks. Often under oaks.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.edibility_detail: Good edible reputation with correct ID; still not a beginner shortcut.

records.field_identification: Dark blackish velvety cap, dark stem, white pore surface becoming pinkish, and bruising that turns red then brown/black are the key field marks. Often under oaks.

records.culinary_uses: Edible reputation exists, but this guide treats it as a cautious ID-first species because blue-staining boletes need care.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Straw-colored brittle bolete with dramatic blue staining and a yellow spore print; usually in sandy hardwood-edge habitats.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Straw-yellow cap and stem, brittle/chalky texture, strong blue staining when cut or bruised, and yellow spore print separate it from most boletes.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.edibility_detail: Use only with confident ID; not for casual beginner harvesting.

records.culinary_uses: Potentially good porcini-type edible with confident ID, but the North American edulis group is messy and should be handled conservatively.
records.notes: Needs review: needs real field photo / image coverage, needs source links, and needs species-specific notes review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern porcini-group bolete with wrinkled grayish-brown cap, hardwood association, white flesh, and a magenta/purple ammonia reaction on the cap.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Porcini-type build, wrinkled or pocked grayish-brown cap, white pores becoming yellow/olive, prominent stem reticulation, white non-staining flesh, and hardwood association. Chemical reaction can help separate it from nearby edulis-like species.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
pore_color: White pores becoming yellow/olive; not bruising

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Boletus Variipes-Fagicola. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall under oaks and other hardwoods.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
pore_color: White young becoming yellowish to olive

records.edibility_detail: Confirm hardwood/oak habitat, dry tan-gray cap, white pores becoming yellowish/olive without bruising, and prominent whitish reticulation on the stem.
records.field_identification: Dry tan to grayish-brown cap, white young pore surface becoming yellowish or olive, non-bruising flesh and pores, and a usually prominent whitish reticulation over a brownish stem under eastern hardwoods.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus variipes
use_links.notes: Oak/hardwood association, non-bruising pores, and reticulate stem.

mushroom_profile.summary: Odd oak-associated bolete with soft brown cap, dull yellow boletinoid pores running slightly down the stem, and brown-to-reddish bruising.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall east of the Rocky Mountains.
research_notes: Soft brown cap, dull yellow pore surface that begins to run down the stem, slow brown to reddish-brown bruising, and brown reticulation at the stem apex. Usually associated with oaks.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

staining: Pores bruise brown; flesh may slowly stain pinkish

records.edibility_detail: Taste should be mild rather than bitter. Pinkish-tan pores and brown bruising can cause confusion with other Tylopilus species.
records.field_identification: Oak-associated woodland Tylopilus with a maroon to dark reddish-brown velvety cap, whitish pores becoming dingy pinkish-tan, pores bruising brown, and a mild/sweet taste rather than bitter.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Tylopilus badiceps
use_links.notes: Oak association, mild taste, pore color, and brown bruising.

pore_color: White young becoming yellowish to olive

reviewReasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.edibility_detail: Confirm hardwood/oak habitat, dry tan-gray cap, white pores becoming yellowish/olive without bruising, and prominent whitish reticulation on the stem.
records.field_identification: Dry tan to grayish-brown cap, white young pore surface becoming yellowish or olive, non-bruising flesh and pores, and a usually prominent whitish reticulation over a brownish stem under eastern hardwoods.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus variipes
use_links.notes: Oak/hardwood association, non-bruising pores, and reticulate stem.

Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.edibility_detail: Treat as a group record. Yellowish-brown velvety caps and variable blue bruising are not enough by themselves for beginner-level edibility confidence.
records.field_identification: Yellowish to brown finely velvety cap, yellow to olive-yellow pores, sometimes blue bruising pores or flesh, hardwood association, and a stem that may show reddish granules and ribbing near the apex.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Xerocomus subtomentosus aggregate
use_links.notes: Hardwood association, yellow pores, reddish granules, ribbing, and variable blue staining.
